description: Fire is called the mouth of the gods, and these eight verses from the Agni Purana address it as exactly that. The Agni Ashtakam names Agni in his many forms, Vaishvanara, Jataveda, Jatharagni, the same flame that cooks food and carries offerings skyward. Devotees turn to it to purify body, mind, and karma, and to strengthen the digestive fire and overall vitality. Tuesday is the usual day for chanting, and many recite it during Holi, when bonfires already mark the season. Eight verses move quickly; the whole reading takes about four minutes, short enough to fold into a morning routine. Here you will find the full text in Devanagari, a Latin transliteration to guide pronunciation, the Hindi arth explaining each line, and a PDF you can download and keep for chanting offline.

Eight fierce guardians, one prayer. The Ashta Bhairava Stotram calls on all eight Bhairava forms of Lord Shiva, drawing its verses from the Rudrayamala Tantra. People recite it when fear closes in, against ghosts, evil spirits, and black magic, and to ask instead for health, courage, knowledge, and a settled fearlessness. It is also chanted to clear away enemies and the obstacles they raise. Tuesday and Saturday carry the strongest charge for this one, and Kalashtami and Maha Shivaratri are the festival days when devotees return to it. The text runs eight verses, roughly eight minutes of chanting, which keeps it within reach for a daily habit rather than a rare occasion. On this page you can read the complete stotram in Devanagari, with Latin transliteration for the sounds, the Hindi meaning line by line, and a free PDF to download for offline recitation.

Pilgrims to Sabarimala carry this prayer like a shield. The Ayyappa Kavacham, drawn from the Shasta Tantra, is the divine armor of Lord Ayyappa, covering every part of the body and every direction with Shasta's protective power. It belongs especially to the Mandala Kalam vratam, the long austerity that precedes the climb, and devotees recite it hoping for jyoti-darshanam, the vision of the divine light at the shrine. Saturday is the day most associated with it, and Mandala Puja and Makar Sankranti are its festival anchors. Seven verses make for a short recitation, about four minutes, easy to keep up daily through the vow period. The full kavacham appears here in Devanagari, alongside a plain transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a downloadable PDF for chanting away from the screen.

This is the chapter where Krishna explains why he keeps returning. Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga, the fourth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita within the Mahabharata, holds the famous declaration Yada Yada Hi Dharmasya and reveals the secret of divine incarnation, the Avatar Rahasya. Across its 42 verses Krishna untangles action, inaction, and forbidden action, and teaches that true knowledge burns away karmic bondage the way fire consumes fuel. Wednesday is the day devotees favor for it, and Janmashtami is its natural festival. A full reading takes around eighteen minutes, longer than a short stotra but worth the sitting. The chapter is laid out here in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to follow the chant, the Hindi meaning unpacking each shloka, and a free PDF for offline study and recitation.

Struck down on the battlefield, Bhishma still saw beauty. The Bhishma Stuti, from Canto 1, Chapter 9 of the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, is his dying prayer, his mind fixed on Krishna's divine form even as life left him. It is counted among the most loved compositions in Vaishnava literature, partly because it teaches the hardest thing of all, how to hold God's image in the mind at the moment of departure. Devotees recite it for that vision of Krishna and for the gift of unwavering devotion at the end. Wednesday and Thursday suit it, and it returns each Janmashtami and Gita Jayanti. Eleven verses take roughly ten minutes. The complete stuti is given here in Devanagari, with transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a downloadable PDF for recitation offline.

When the Moon sits weak or troubled in a birth chart, this is the kavach traditionally prescribed. The Chandra Kavacham, gathered in the Graha Stotra Sangraha, is protective armor against Chandra-related afflictions in the horoscope. Beyond the astrological side, devotees chant it to calm a restless mind, ease stress and anxiety, and ask for the kind of charm and moonlike radiance the planet governs. Monday is its day, the weekday that belongs to the Moon, and it is also recited during Sharad Purnima and Karva Chauth. The text is brief, seven verses and about three minutes, which makes daily repetition effortless. You will find the full kavacham here in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the sounds, the Hindi meaning for each verse, and a free PDF you can download for offline chanting.

The name says it plainly: daridrya dahana, the burning away of poverty. This eight-verse hymn to Shiva, traditionally attributed to Vaishampayan Rishi, is recited squarely against want, the sorrows that trail it, and the weight of financial obstacles and debts. The promise held in the tradition is gradual rather than sudden, daily chanting is said to bring steady improvement in one's financial condition, and to invite wealth, prosperity, and material abundance over time. Monday is the day for it, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan as the high points. At eight verses it reads in about five minutes, fitting both quiet daily worship and special days. The complete stotram is presented here in Devanagari, with transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi arth, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

A mother's prayer at the birth of her son, who happened to be God. The Devaki Stuti, from chapter 3 of the tenth canto of the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, gives voice to Devaki as she beholds the newborn Krishna in the prison cell. It draws out the maternal grace of the Lord and deepens one's grasp of his divine birth. Devotees recite it to lift fear and anxiety from the heart and to seek protection from powerful enemies, the very threat Devaki herself faced. Wednesday and Saturday are its days, and it belongs above all to Janmashtami. Nine verses run about seven minutes. The full stuti is set out here in Devanagari, with a clear transliteration, the Hindi meaning verse by verse, and a free PDF for chanting offline.

Healing has a god, and this is his armor. The Dhanvantari Kavacham, from the Agni Purana, invokes Lord Dhanvantari, the celestial physician, as a shield over every part of the body. The classical tradition recites it to drive out disease and to ask for a full hundred years of life, and devotees consider it especially potent for chronic and stubborn ailments that resist ordinary remedy. Beyond protection of health, it is chanted for general victory and well-being. Tuesday and Thursday are the favored days, and Dhanteras, the festival tied to Dhanvantari's emergence, is its natural occasion. Seven verses take around seven minutes, easy enough to keep as a daily practice. Here the complete kavacham appears in Devanagari, accompanied by a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Not one Gayatri but three. The Durga Gayatri Mantra, drawn from the Durga Saptashati of the Markandeya Purana, gathers three variations addressing the Goddess as Katyayani, Mahadevi, and Girija. It is a Vedic-style meditation mantra meant to free the devotee from calamities and dangers, and a strong protective formula, particularly favored on Tuesdays and Fridays and across the nine nights of Navratri. People also chant it to clear away enemies and negative forces pressing on their lives. The set runs five short verses, about four minutes in all, which makes it practical for daily repetition. On this page the full mantra is given in Devanagari, with a plain transliteration to fix the pronunciation, the Hindi meaning of each line, and a free PDF to download and chant offline. Durga Puja is its other great festival occasion.

Reciting it is said to equal the journey to Dwarka itself. The Dwarkadhish Stotram, from the Bhagavata Purana, praises Krishna as the lord of Dwarka and carries the promise of pilgrimage merit without the road. Devotees chant it seeking Krishna-sayujya, union with the Lord, and ultimately Goloka, his supreme abode, while trusting the same protection Krishna extends to all who surrender to him. Wednesday and Friday are the days it suits best, and Janmashtami and Ekadashi are when devotees return to it. Ten verses take roughly seven minutes, an easy fit for a daily routine. The complete stotram is laid out here in Devanagari script, with a Latin transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi arth explaining each verse, and a downloadable PDF so you can recite it offline whenever you wish.

Five jewels, one elephant-headed lord. Adi Shankaracharya composed the Ganesh Pancharatnam, and its five verses are held to be unusually powerful and meritorious. The blessings it names are wide, arogata or freedom from illness, freedom from defects, eloquence, good progeny, long life, and the ashta-aishvarya, the eight forms of prosperity. It is also chanted to cut through obstacles, the antaraya-krintanam, that block both spiritual and worldly pursuits, and the tradition holds that reciting it in the morning brings desires to fulfillment quickly. Wednesday is Ganesha's weekday, and Ganesh Chaturthi is its festival. The five verses take about seven minutes to read with care. You will find the complete pancharatnam here in Devanagari, with transliteration for the sounds, the Hindi meaning line by line, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

One hundred and eight names, each opening a door. The Gayatri Ashtottara Shatanamavali, from the Devi Bhagavata Purana, is a namavali for archana worship, where every name is offered with a flower or a grain of rice to a different aspect of Gayatri Shakti. Devotees recite it for supreme knowledge and spiritual illumination, and the tradition holds that it carries all siddhis, moksha, and the Mother's full blessings. Sunday and Wednesday are its days, and Gayatri Jayanti is its festival. The recitation of all 108 names takes around eight minutes, steady and meditative rather than hurried. On this page the complete namavali appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to follow each name correctly, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a downloadable PDF for archana and chanting offline.

Before the Gita itself, this praises the Gita. The Gita Mahatmya, preserved in the Padma Purana, describes the supreme glory and merit of reading the Bhagavad Gita, going so far as to say that even half a verse, a third, or a single line yields immense reward. The tradition holds that Lord Vishnu himself resides wherever the Gita is recited, and that this reading washes away all sins, including the maha-papas, the way water rolls off a lotus leaf. Wednesday, Thursday, and Saturday are its days, with Gita Jayanti and Ekadashi as festival occasions. Seventeen verses take about eight minutes. The complete text is given here in Devanagari, with transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

One short prayer to settle all nine planets at once. The Graha Peeda Hara Stotram comes out of Jyotish Shastra and is recited to pacify the entire Navagraha together rather than one graha at a time. It addresses every kind of graha dosha and planetary affliction, and devotees lean on it during difficult dasha and antardasha periods, when the planetary clock turns heavy. It is also chanted simply to have wishes fulfilled through the grace of the nine. Sunday and Saturday are its days, the weekdays of Surya and Shani. The text is compact, six verses and about three minutes, which makes it ideal for daily planetary pacification. Here the full stotram is set out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline chanting.

Begin any Hanuman worship by picturing him, and these are the verses for it. The Hanuman Dhyanam gathers the most beloved dhyana shlokas from the Hanuman Chalisa tradition and the Valmiki Ramayana, including Atulita Baladhamam, Manojavam, and the Yatra Yatra verses, and even the Hanuman Gayatri for deeper meditation. The smarana, the remembrance of Hanuman, is said to bring eight divine qualities, wisdom, strength, fame, courage, fearlessness, health, vigor, and eloquence. Tuesday, Saturday, and daily practice all suit it, with Hanuman Jayanti as its festival high point. The eight verses take roughly five minutes to chant. On this page the complete dhyanam appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to fix the sounds, the Hindi meaning of each shloka, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Every night at Sabarimala, the sanctum doors close to this song. Harivarasanam, composed by Kambangudi Kulathur Srinivasa Iyer, is the lullaby sung as Lord Ayyappa is put to rest, the single most sacred Ayyappa devotional in the tradition. It addresses him as Hariharaputra, son of both Vishnu in the form of Mohini and Shiva, and is recited to lift the fear of worldly existence, the bhava-bhaya, while granting the wishes and aspirations of devotees. Saturday is its weekday, and Makara Vilakku and Mandala Puja are its festival seasons. Eight verses take about six minutes, suitable for daily singing as much as for temple closing. Here the complete song is presented in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to follow the Malayalam-rooted lyrics, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline use.

Five gem-verses for the lord of Puri. The Jagannath Pancharatnam, drawn from the Skanda Purana, is a compact set of praises to Lord Jagannath in all his forms, recited for devotion and steady spiritual growth. Devotees chant it for divine protection and auspiciousness, and the tradition holds that it clears away sins and bestows spiritual merit. Thursday is the day most associated with it, and Rath Yatra and Janmashtami are the festivals when it is most often heard. With only five verses, the recitation takes about three minutes, brief enough to slip into daily worship without strain. On this page you will find the complete pancharatnam in Devanagari, accompanied by a Latin transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

The hridayam is the heart, and this prayer claims to reveal the very heart of Kali worship. Drawn from the Bhairava Tantra, the Kali Hridayam holds that mere knowledge of it makes the sadhaka Shiva-like. It carries the sacred bija-traya, the three seed-syllables Krim, Klim, Hum, central to Kali upasana, and the tradition promises all siddhis, the destruction of every sin, and a path leading to Parabrahma. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, and Kali Puja, Diwali, and Navratri its festivals. The fourteen verses take around ten minutes to recite. Here the complete hridayam is laid out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to fix the mantras precisely, the Hindi arth explaining each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline chanting wherever you sit to practice.

Shankaracharya begged for a poor woman, and gold rained down. The Kanakadhara Stotram carries that story in its name, kanakadhara meaning a stream of gold, and Adi Shankaracharya's twenty-one verses are recited above all to dissolve poverty when chanted with real devotion. Devotees turn to it for the grace and blessings of Goddess Lakshmi, for the fulfillment of material desires, and for that legendary shower of wealth the story promises. Friday is Lakshmi's weekday, and Diwali is the festival when households most often recite it. The twenty-one verses take roughly twelve minutes, a fuller sitting than most stotras. On this page the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi meaning for each verse, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Haare Ka Sahara, the support of the defeated, is what devotees call him. The Khatu Shyam Chalisa praises Krishna in his Khatu Shyam form, the deity many regard as the lord of Kaliyug who answers the prayers of the downtrodden. The forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, chalis meaning forty, and the framing dohas bring the full recitation to forty-three verse-units, counted here as 42 verses. People recite it when hope has run out, for delayed marriages, job troubles, and money problems, and for the justice it is said to bring those who stay truthful. Sunday is its day, with special weight during the Phalguna Mela at Khatu Dham and on Shyam Ekadashi. The reading takes about fifteen minutes. The full chalisa is given here in Devanagari, with transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a downloadable PDF.

Twelve names of the keeper of the gods' treasury. The Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram, from the Vishnu Purana, lists twelve names of Kubera, the celestial treasurer, as a short prayer for wealth. Reciting it is said to drive out poverty and financial difficulty, draw the nava-nidhi, the nine treasures, toward the devotee, and keep Lakshmi steady within the household so prosperity does not slip away. Thursday and Friday are the days for it, with Dhanteras and Diwali as the festival occasions when wealth prayers are most active. Five verses make for a brief recitation, around three minutes, which fits both daily worship and the festival days. On this page the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide pronunciation, the Hindi meaning of each name, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Eight verses, eight forms of fortune. The Lakshmi Mangalashtakam, drawn from the Lakshmi Tantra, invokes the eight forms of Lakshmi, the Ashta Lakshmi, for prosperity that touches every corner of life. Devotees recite it for wealth, fortune, and auspiciousness across all their endeavors, and to ask that Lakshmi take up permanent residence in the home rather than visit and depart. It also addresses financial difficulty and poverty directly. Friday is its weekday, and it suits Diwali, Varalakshmi Vrata, and Sharad Purnima especially well. The eight verses take about five minutes to chant, which keeps it within reach for daily worship as much as for the big festival days. Here the complete ashtakam is set out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration for the sounds, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Not one mantra but a working set of them. The Maha Ganesh Mantra Collection gathers the most powerful Ganesha mantras from various Tantric and Puranic sources into a single practice. Among them, the Rinaharta mantra is aimed squarely at clearing debts and financial burdens, the Siddhi Vinayaka mantra at accomplishing tasks and removing every obstacle, and the Sankat Nashan mantra at destroying crises and dangers of all kinds. Wednesday is Ganesha's weekday, and Ganesh Chaturthi and Sankashti Chaturthi are its festival occasions. The collection runs twelve verse-units and takes around eight minutes to chant through. On this page you will find the complete set in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration so each mantra is pronounced correctly, the Hindi meaning explaining what each one does, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

For anyone wrestling with Mangal in the chart, this is the standard remedy. The Mangal Stotra, from the Skanda Purana, addresses the afflictions of Mars, the Mangal or Kuja dosha, and is recited to relieve the debts, bad luck, and poverty that can trail a difficult Mars placement. It also speaks to Manglik dosha, the affliction that complicates marriage prospects, while asking Mars for what he gives at his best, courage, valor, and fighting spirit. Tuesday, the weekday ruled by Mars, is its day. The text runs eight verses and about five minutes, suited equally to quiet daily worship and to special occasions. Here the complete stotra is presented in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to fix the pronunciation, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Mehandipur Balaji is the shrine people visit when nothing else has worked. Its principal aarti, kept alive by the temple tradition in Rajasthan, addresses Hanuman as Balaji in his fierce protective form, standing alongside Bhairav Baba. The tradition holds it as a strong guard against negative energies, the evil eye, and malefic planetary effects, and the temple is famous for relief from every kind of badha, the spiritual disturbances that the aarti is recited to lift, granting fearlessness in their place. Tuesday and Saturday are Hanuman's days, with Hanuman Jayanti as the festival occasion. Just four verses, about four minutes, make it easy to chant regularly. On this page the complete aarti appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to follow the words, the Hindi meaning of each line, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Ugram Viram Mahavishnum, the line itself is a shield. The Narasimha Panchamrita Stotram, from the Narasimha Purana, is the five-nectar hymn of the man-lion avatar, woven together with a vandana to nine of his sacred kshetras. It carries that famous protection mantra and is recited to wipe away sins and obtain every siddhi, while the tradition counts reciting it as merit equal to visiting all the holy tirthas at once. Saturday is its weekday, and Narasimha Jayanti and Narasimha Chaturdashi are its festivals. The eight verses take about four minutes, short enough for daily chanting. Here the complete stotram is laid out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to pronounce the fierce syllables correctly, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline recitation.

I am not the mind, not the senses, not even the ego, and so it goes, verse after verse. The Nirvana Shatakam, also called Atma Shatakam, is Adi Shankaracharya's six-verse summit of Advaita Vedanta, a sustained neti-neti that strips away every false identification with body, senses, and self. What remains, the composition insists, is the one truth, that I am pure consciousness and bliss, Chidananda. It is recited as a meditation on Self-realization rather than for worldly gain, freeing the mind from its attachments. Monday is its weekday, with Maha Shivaratri as its festival. Six verses take around four minutes to chant. On this page the complete shatakam appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the recitation, the Hindi meaning unpacking each negation, and a free PDF for offline study and chanting.

Standing on a brick at Pandharpur, hands on hips, that is the Vitthal these verses adore. The Pandurangashtakam, composed by Adi Shankaracharya, is an eight-verse hymn to Lord Vitthal, also called Panduranga, the form of Vishnu worshipped at Pandharpur. Devotees recite it to burn away great sins and purify the soul, to lift mental anguish and sorrow, and ultimately to taste the experience of Parabrahma through pure devotion, with liberation, mukti, held out to the devoted reader. Wednesday and Thursday are its days, and Ashadhi Ekadashi and Kartiki Ekadashi, the great Pandharpur pilgrimage days, are its festivals. The eight verses take about eight minutes. Here the complete ashtakam is given in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

A single page of the shlokas a child first learns at home. The Prarthana Shloka Sangraha pulls together the essential daily verses for all the major deities, gathered from various scriptures, including Sarve Bhavantu Sukhinah, the line that radiates universal goodwill and compassion. Across thirteen verses it covers the full range a household turns to, obstacle removal, knowledge, protection, prosperity, with each shloka invoking a specific deity for a specific blessing. It is meant for daily recitation in homes, schools, and temples rather than for any one festival or weekday. The collection reads in about ten minutes. On this page you will find the complete sangraha in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration so the verses can be pronounced correctly, the Hindi meaning of each shloka, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

Jaya Jaya, again and again, the whole life of Rama told as a victory cry. The Raghuveera Gadyam was composed by Vedanta Deshika in the thirteenth to fourteenth century, one of the greatest Vaishnava Acharyas, and it narrates Rama's complete story in powerful prose rather than metered verse. Each Jaya Jaya invocation lands as a declaration of his triumph, and devotees recite it for victory over enemies and obstacles and for the divine courage, strength, and fearlessness Rama embodies. Sunday is its weekday, and Ram Navami and Dussehra are its festivals. The composition runs seven sections and takes about seven minutes to recite aloud. Here the complete gadyam is presented in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to carry the rolling prose, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

One hymn that walks through the whole of Rama's life. The Rama Stotram, drawn from the broader body of traditional Rama literature, is a complete praise covering every aspect of the Lord's story across eleven verses. Devotees recite it for his blessings and protection, and the classical tradition holds that it clears away sins and grants liberation, Rama-sayujya, union with Rama himself. It is also chanted to draw down peace, righteousness, and prosperity into daily life. Thursday and Sunday are its weekdays, and Ram Navami and Dussehra its great festivals. The eleven verses take about twelve minutes to read with attention. On this page the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation whenever you wish.

Shraddha and Saburi, faith and patience, the two words Sai Baba lived by. The Sai Stotram, from the Sai devotional tradition, praises Shirdi Sai Baba across eight verses as Sadguru and divine incarnation, naming those two core teachings and honoring him as an avatar of Dattatreya and a teacher for everyone, regardless of path. Devotees recite it for the fulfillment of wishes, for healing, and for spiritual guidance in everyday difficulties. Thursday, the day of the guru, is its weekday, and Guru Purnima is its festival. The eight verses take about four minutes, brief enough for daily devotion. Here the complete stotram is laid out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to follow the words, the Hindi arth explaining each verse, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline recitation.

Students reach for this one before exams, and with reason. The Saraswati Hridayam, from the Saraswati Rahasya, is the heart-essence prayer to the goddess of learning, recited to bring vidya, knowledge, and buddhi, intelligence, swiftly. It is held to grant eloquence, poetic ability, and command over speech, and to sharpen memory, concentration, and the capacity to learn, which is why it is so often chanted by those preparing for examinations. Thursday and Wednesday are its days, with Vasant Panchami and Saraswati Puja as its festivals. The eight verses take about seven minutes to recite. On this page you will find the complete hridayam in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration for accurate pronunciation, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF you can download and use for offline recitation.

Sarva Mangala Mangalye, the verse Hindus recite at nearly every ceremony, lives at the center of this hymn. The Sarva Mangala Stotram, from the Durga Saptashati within the Markandeya Purana, is among the most auspicious of all, heard at weddings, pujas, and festivals alike. The tradition holds that the Goddess removes fear, disease, calamity, and affliction at her mere remembrance, and grants beauty, victory, and fame while destroying enemies. Friday and Tuesday are its weekdays, and Navratri, Dussehra, and Durga Puja its festivals. The ten verses take about six minutes, suited to daily worship as much as to grand occasions. Here the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Even Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva, and Indra answer to Shani, and this hymn says so plainly. The Shani Mahatmya Stotram, drawn from the Skanda Purana, recounts the supreme power and glory of Lord Shani across twelve verses. The teaching it carries is practical, that understanding Shani's greatness and worshipping him with genuine devotion is what wards off his affliction, since he rewards the devoted and punishes the wicked. Reciting it is the way devotees ask for his grace rather than his wrath. Saturday, Shani's own weekday, is its day, with Shani Jayanti and Shani Amavasya as its festivals. The twelve verses take about seven minutes. On this page the complete stotram is given in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Avinayam Apanaya Vishno, remove my arrogance, O Vishnu, the prayer opens, and the request sets its whole tone. The Shatpadi Stotram is Adi Shankaracharya's six-verse masterpiece of Vishnu devotion, recited to dissolve pride, tame the restless mind, and quiet worldly desires. It widens compassion toward all beings and is held to liberate the devotee from Samsara, and it contains the lovely wave-and-ocean image for the relationship between the Jiva and Brahman, the wave never apart from the sea. Thursday is its weekday, and Guru Purnima its festival. The six verses take about five minutes to chant. Here the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the recitation, the Hindi arth explaining each verse, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline use.

Five short verses, each opening with Om Namah Shivaya. The Shiva Panchakam, from the Shiva Purana, is a compact and potent praise of Lord Shiva, built around the Panchakshara, the five-syllable mantra that begins every verse. Devotees recite it to surrender the self completely to Parameshvara, to purify the mind, and to settle into inner peace. The tradition holds that steady reciters reach Shivaloka, the abode of Shiva. Monday is Shiva's weekday, and Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan are its festival seasons. With only five verses it takes about three minutes, which makes daily chanting effortless. On this page you will find the complete panchakam in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration so the Panchakshara is pronounced correctly, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Start a new venture, and many will chant this first. The Shiva Mangalashtakam, from the Shiva Purana, calls down the auspicious blessings of Lord Shiva across eight verses, and is traditionally recited at the opening of any fresh undertaking or auspicious occasion. It is held to draw all-round success, sarvartha siddhi, and to wrap a protective, auspicious aura around the devotee, alongside peace, prosperity, and Shiva's blessing. Monday is Shiva's weekday, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan as its festival times. The eight verses take about five minutes to recite, fitting both daily worship and special days. Here the complete ashtakam is laid out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to fix the pronunciation, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline recitation.

It ends with the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, and that tells you where it is headed. The Shri Hari Stuti gathers the most beloved Vishnu and Krishna verses, drawn from the Bhagavad Gita, the Vishnu Purana, and the wider Vaishnava tradition, into a single universal praise of Hari. Among its eight verses sits the Bhagavad Gita's Charama Shloka, 18.66, the supreme verse of surrender, and the universal prayer Sarve Bhavantu Sukhinah, before it closes into nama sankeertana with the Maha Mantra. It is meant for daily recitation, with Janmashtami, Vaikuntha Ekadashi, and Tulsi Vivah as its festival occasions. The reading takes about four minutes. On this page the complete stuti appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

The ninth night of Navratri belongs to her. The Siddhidatri Stotram, from the traditional Navdurga stotras, praises the ninth and final form of the Goddess, worshipped on Day 9. She is the giver of siddhis, and the hymn is recited to obtain all eight supernatural powers, anima, mahima, garima and the rest, along with supreme spiritual realization and paramananda, the highest bliss. The tradition recalls that even Brahma, Vishnu, and Mahesh received their siddhis from her. Tuesday and Friday are its weekdays, and Navratri is its festival. The eleven verses take about five minutes to recite. Here the complete stotram is given in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi arth explaining each verse, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline recitation through the nine nights.

Among the oldest of all Lakshmi prayers, this one comes from the Veda itself. The Sri Suktam belongs to the Rig Veda Khilani, the supplementary hymns, and is recited for devotion and steady spiritual growth as much as for worldly fortune. Devotees chant its seven verses to invoke the blessings of Lakshmi in all her forms, to draw divine protection and auspiciousness, and the tradition holds that it removes sins and bestows spiritual merit. Friday is Lakshmi's weekday, and Diwali, Akshaya Tritiya, and Dhanteras are its festival occasions. The seven verses take about five minutes to chant, suited to daily worship and the festival days alike. On this page the complete suktam appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to carry the Vedic accents, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

A prayer for first light. The Surya Pancharatnam, composed by Adi Shankaracharya, is a set of five jewel-verses meant for morning worship of the Sun. It addresses Surya as the Adi Purusha, the primordial being and source of all life, and devotees recite it for freedom from diseases and sufferings and for knowledge, health, and spiritual illumination, the inner light to match the outer. Sunday, the Sun's own weekday, is its day, and Makar Sankranti and Ratha Saptami are its festivals. With only five verses, the recitation takes about three minutes, easy to fold into the dawn routine. Here the complete pancharatnam is laid out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline recitation at sunrise.

The seventh Jyotirlinga has its own hymn, and this is it. The Trimbakeshwar Stotram, from the Shiva Purana, is recited as a stand-in for pilgrimage to Trimbakeshwar, near the source of the Godavari, and the tradition counts its merit as equal to the Kumbh Mela bath when chanted with devotion. It is the place tied to the vibrations of the Mahamrityunjaya mantra, and the stotram is chanted to conquer the fear of death, Mrityunjaya, and to seek longevity and freedom from disease, leading finally to Shiva-sayujya and eternal liberation. Monday is its weekday, with Maha Shivaratri, Shravan Somvar, and Kumbh Mela as its festivals. The eleven verses take about eight minutes. On this page the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Sing it at the cave shrine in the hills, or sing it at home, the tradition treats both with the same warmth. The Vaishno Devi Aarti is the essential aarti for the Goddess at her temple and in household worship, calling on the triple blessings of Mahakali, Mahalakshmi, and Mahasaraswati who together make up Vaishno Devi. Devotees recite it for prosperity, happiness, and the well-being of the family, and the tradition holds that singing it with devotion removes sins and purifies the singer. Tuesday and Friday are its weekdays, and Navratri and the Vaishno Devi Yatra are its festival occasions. The nine verses take about five minutes to sing. Here the complete aarti is given in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to follow the words, the Hindi arth for each line, and a downloadable PDF for offline use.

One god, three lives, sung by the acharya who claimed to be the third. Madhvacharya composed the Vayu Stuti, praising Vayu Deva across his three incarnations, Hanuman in the Ramayana age, Bhimasena in the Mahabharata, and Madhvacharya himself as the teacher of Dvaita. It sits at the center of the Madhva Vaishnava tradition and is recited daily by followers of the Madhva Sampradaya. Devotees chant its eight verses for strength, knowledge, and unwavering devotion to Vishnu. Tuesday and Saturday are its weekdays, and Madhva Navami is its festival. The recitation takes about five minutes. On this page you will find the complete stuti in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration to guide the dense Sanskrit, the Hindi meaning of each verse, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

The divine architect blesses the workshop, and engineers and artisans keep this on their lips on his day. The Vishwakarma Chalisa, from traditional Vishwakarma bhakti literature, is recited for mastery and skill in craftsmanship and engineering, for the safety of workers and artisans against workplace accidents, and for success in business and industry. It is also chanted to bless tools and machinery for safe operation, a fitting prayer for factory floors and family trades. The forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, chalis meaning forty, and the framing dohas bring it to forty-three verse-units, counted here as 10 verses. Sunday is its weekday, and Vishwakarma Jayanti its festival. The reading takes about twelve minutes. Here the complete chalisa appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.
